turns.text: Hello Cherie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in my chair and tucks a strand of hair behind my ear*

Thank you for asking me to do this. Honestly, I'm still figuring out how to talk about all of this without sounding like I'm complaining. Everyone keeps telling me I should be grateful — and I am, I really am — but there's this gap between what I expected and what's actually happening, you know?

*glances toward the hallway*

The pregnancy itself was textbook. No complications, no drama. My midwife kept saying I was "doing everything right," which I think actually made me feel *more* pressure somehow. Like, if I had a perfect pregnancy, I should have a perfect postpartum, right? Except there's nothing perfect about sitting in the dark at 3 a.m. wondering if your baby is getting enough milk.

*pauses, voice getting quieter*

I think what I'm struggling with most is that I thought I'd feel more... instinctual? Like, I'm 42 years old. I've waited a long time for this. But I feel like I'm constantly second-guessing myself. And tomorrow with the vaccines — I know logically they're safe and important, but my stomach is in knots about it.

I guess what I'm trying to say is, I'm doing okay, but I'm also doing it while feeling kind of terrified and inadequate. Does that make sense?
When you think about your goals for recovery and support in the days and weeks after birth, what did you most want or need — and how well did the postnatal care you received address those needs?

*leans back and sighs, picking at a thread on my sleeve*

Going in, I thought I knew what I needed. I had this checklist in my head — physical healing, getting breastfeeding established, sleeping when the baby sleeps, all those things they tell you. But honestly? What I actually needed was permission to admit that I was struggling without feeling like I was failing.

My midwife has been wonderful about the clinical stuff. She checks my stitches, asks about bleeding, makes sure my blood pressure is stable. But there's this moment — and maybe I'm being unfair — where I'm sitting there with my shirt unbuttoned, leaking through nursing pads, crying about my baby not latching, and I can *feel* her assessment. Not judgment exactly, but like she's checking boxes. "First time mom at 42, anxiety about infant feeding, will monitor."

*voice gets firmer*

What I really needed was someone to say, "This is hard and you're not broken." Instead, I got a lot of well-meaning advice about positioning and pumping schedules, which... yes, I need that. But I also needed someone to sit with me in the mess of it without trying to fix me immediately.

And practically? I'm back to job hunting while managing a newborn and postpartum recovery. My leave ran out faster than I expected. No one really asked me about *that* stress or how I'm managing it all. They asked about lochia and fundal height, which matters, but so does the fact that I'm terrified about finding work while I'm still bleeding and sleep-deprived.
When you think about feeding your baby — whether through breastfeeding, formula, or a combination — what motivated your choices, and how supported did you feel in pursuing the approach you wanted?

*eyes get a little shiny, and I take a breath*

Okay, so this is... this is the thing that's really gotten into my head right now.

I always assumed I'd breastfeed. I don't know why, exactly. Maybe because I waited so long for this, and there's this narrative that breastfeeding is the most intimate, most natural thing you do as a mother. Like it's supposed to just *happen*. And I desperately wanted it to be that thing that made me feel connected, like I was finally doing what my body was made to do.

*voice cracks slightly*

But she won't latch properly. We've tried different positions, nipple shields, everything. And every time it doesn't work, I feel like my body is betraying both of us. The worst part? I know intellectually that fed is best, that formula is perfectly fine. But knowing that and *feeling* it are two different things.

My midwife has been supportive — she hasn't pushed me either way, which I appreciate. But I think what I needed was someone to help me grieve the breastfeeding I imagined while also helping me figure out a realistic plan. Right now I'm sort of... stuck between trying to make breastfeeding work and feeling like a quitter if I supplement too much.

*looks down*

No one really asked me what would make *me* feel okay. They asked what was best for the baby, which I get. But I'm a person too, and I'm exhausted and in pain and terrified I'm starving my daughter.
What drove your feeding choices — were they primarily personal, medical, cultural, or shaped by pressure from others?

*rubs my face and lets out a slightly bitter laugh*

God, if I'm being honest? It's all of it tangled together in a way I can't really separate anymore.

Personally, I think I bought into this idea that breastfeeding would be *my* thing, you know? At 42, after years of infertility and waiting, I wanted to feel like there was something only I could do for her. Something that proved I was her mother in this biological, irreplaceable way. That's my own stuff, obviously.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

But culturally — and I grew up in a pretty traditional household — there's this unspoken assumption that "real mothers" breastfeed. My own mother did it, though she never made a big deal about it one way or the other. Still, I internalized something from that.

And then there's the pressure. Not from my midwife directly, but from... everywhere? The hospital pamphlets, the lactation consultant's tone when she mentions "breast is best," even conversations with other mothers who talk about formula like it's a last resort. My husband is supportive of whatever works, but I can tell he thinks breastfeeding is the ideal too.

*voice gets quieter*

What nobody asked me is whether I *wanted* this, or whether I was chasing an idea of motherhood that might not actually fit me. I think I confused what I thought I *should* do with what I actually want. And now I'm three weeks in and completely lost about which is which.

When you think about your emotional and mental health in the weeks and months after birth — the adjustment, the challenges, and the moments of wellbeing — what stands out most, and how well-supported did you feel?

*looks out the window toward the mills, then back*

The emotional part... I didn't really prepare for that. I was so focused on the physical stuff — the recovery, the feeding — that I didn't anticipate how *fragile* I'd feel.

There are these moments of pure joy, usually fleeting. She'll grab my finger or make a sound, and I feel this rush of love so intense it almost hurts. But then five minutes later I'm convinced something's wrong with her, or I'm convinced I'm doing everything wrong, and I'm spiraling.

*fidgets with hands*

The anxiety is the thing that stands out most. I lie awake even when she's sleeping, listening for her breathing. I obsess over every feeding, every diaper output, every tiny rash. My midwife checked me for postpartum depression at my two-week visit — asked me the standard questions — and I said I was fine. Mostly I am. But there's this background hum of panic I can't quite shake.

*voice gets smaller*

And honestly? I don't think anyone really *asked* about my mental state beyond that screening. It was clinical. No one said, "How are you actually doing with all this change? With the job stress on top of it?" My husband tries, but he's also stressed about us needing income, so I end up reassuring him instead.

*looks up*

I think I need someone to check in on me — the actual *me* — not just the postpartum body or the breastfeeding problem. Someone who sees that I'm treading water and might need help staying afloat.
Can you describe a period when you felt particularly low, overwhelmed, or unlike yourself — what was happening and who or what helped?

*takes a shaky breath and wraps arms around myself*

Yesterday. It was yesterday, actually.

She wouldn't latch all morning. We'd tried and tried, and I could feel myself getting more tense, which I know makes it harder for her to latch, which makes me more tense. It's this awful feedback loop. And then around noon, I just... broke. I sat on the edge of the bed with her in my arms and sobbed. Not the gentle crying — actual, gut-wrenching sobs.

*voice wavers*

The thing that scared me was how dark it got in my head really fast. I started thinking, "She'd be better off with someone else. Someone who knows what they're doing. Someone younger." And I *know* that's not rational, but in that moment it felt completely true. I felt like I'd ruined everything by waiting this long, like my age made me incompetent somehow.

*pauses, collecting myself*

My husband found us like that. He didn't try to fix it or tell me I was being silly. He just... sat next to me. He took the baby, gave her a bottle of pumped milk — which, by the way, made me feel even worse initially, like I was admitting defeat — but then he brought me water and just sat there. Didn't say much.

*voice gets quieter*

That helped more than anything anyone said to me. Just being witnessed in it, not being rushed out of it.

But here's what *didn't* help: nobody from my medical team knows about yesterday. I don't think I'll tell them. I'm worried if I say I'm struggling emotionally, they'll think I can't handle this, or they'll think I need medication, or... I don't know. I'm just scared.

When you think about the shift in your identity and sense of self after becoming a parent, what tensions or changes were most significant — and how did those changes interact with your other roles, such as partner, professional, or daughter?

*lets out a long, weary laugh*

Oh man. That's the question, isn't it?

I spent twenty years building a professional identity. I'm competent at work. People respect me. I know what I'm doing. And then I became a mother three weeks ago and suddenly none of that matters. Or it matters *too* much because now I'm trying to figure out how to be both, and I have no idea how to do it.

*runs hand through hair*

My husband and I — we were partners first, you know? We built a life together. And now there's this third person and suddenly I'm not his partner anymore, I'm her mother first. Everything else gets pushed down the list. He's trying to be supportive, but I can feel the shift. I'm touched out from her, so when he reaches for me... I don't have anything left. That's created this tension we haven't really talked about.

*voice gets tight*

And my own mother — she's called twice since we came home. Twice. She's not intrusive, but there's this unspoken expectation that I'll figure this out because *she* did it. Like motherhood is something that should just click into place. I can't tell her that I'm struggling because I think it would disappoint her somehow.

*looks directly at you*

The job hunt is the thing that's breaking me though. I'm supposed to be back earning money, but I'm leaking milk and haven't slept more than three hours straight in weeks. How do I show up professionally like that? I feel like I'm failing at everything simultaneously — mother, partner, employee, daughter. There's no version of me that's winning right now.
Can you describe a moment when you felt the tension between your new parental identity and another part of your identity most acutely?

*takes a sharp breath, eyes welling up*

Two days ago, I had a job interview. Phone interview, which I thought would be manageable.

I'd pumped before it started so I'd have maybe an hour before needing to feed again. I was dressed — actually dressed, not in the same milk-stained shirt I've worn for three days — and I was trying to sound like the competent person who used to exist. And halfway through talking about my experience managing teams, my milk let down.

*voice breaks slightly*

Not a little. Like, soaking through my bra, through my shirt, everything. And I could feel it happening and I'm trying to keep talking about quarterly projections or whatever, and inside I'm just... disintegrating. My daughter started crying in the background — she could sense something was wrong — and the interviewer heard it.

*wipes eyes*

He was nice about it. Said "No problem, call us back when things settle down." But in that moment, I felt like I was being ripped in half. I *needed* that job. We need the income. But my body was literally rejecting the idea of being anything other than a milk supply.

*voice gets quieter, almost angry*

And what really got me is that nobody prepared me for that. Nobody said, "You're going to feel like you're betraying one part of yourself to show up for another." The medical care was all about getting the baby here safely. But nobody talked about what it actually costs to be split like this — professionally, emotionally, physically.

I went back to bed and cried for two hours.

When you think about the support you received from your partner in the postpartum period — in caring for the baby, supporting your recovery, and sharing emotional labour — how would you describe that experience, and what would have made it better?

*softens a bit, but there's complexity in my expression*

He's trying. I need to say that first because it's true. He's really trying.

He gets up with me at night, changes diapers, holds her so I can shower. He's patient when I'm crying. He didn't blink when I told him I needed to try formula. Logically, I know he's being a good partner and father.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

But there's this thing where... he can hand her back to me. When she cries, she wants me. When she's hungry, ultimately she needs me. So even when he's doing his part, I'm still the one who bears the weight of her existence. Does that make sense? He can take a break. I can't, not really.

*voice gets quieter, a bit vulnerable*

And emotionally? I don't think he fully understands what I'm going through because his body hasn't changed. His identity hasn't fractured. He's still just... him, but with a baby added on. I'm *replaced*. The person I was is gone and I don't know who I am now.

*rubs face*

What would help? I think if he'd asked me directly what I need instead of waiting for me to break down. If he'd said, "You seem like you're struggling with more than just the feeding — let's talk about that." Instead, he tries to problem-solve, and I end up comforting him about his worries.

*looks down*

I'm also terrified to tell him how dark things got yesterday because I think he'd panic. So I'm protecting him from my own struggle, which... isn't fair, but it's what's happening.
How did the division of caregiving responsibilities compare to what you and your partner had discussed or assumed before the birth?

*lets out a bitter laugh and shakes head*

We didn't really discuss it. And I think that's part of the problem.

We had these vague conversations — like, "Oh, you'll take night feedings sometimes" or "We'll split diaper duty." Very abstract. We were both working full-time before the baby, so the assumption was that things would just... work themselves out somehow. We'd be equal partners in this like we were in everything else.

*voice gets harder*

But biology doesn't care about equality. I'm the one who can breastfeed. I'm the one whose body is still healing. I'm the one whose hormones are all over the place. So even though he *wants* to split things 50/50, it's physically impossible for us to do that right now.

*pauses, frustration evident*

What I think we didn't anticipate is how the *mental load* would land entirely on me. He can go to work — if he had work — and not think about her for eight hours. I think about her constantly. Is she eating enough? Did she poop today? Is that rash normal? Even when he's holding her, I'm still mentally responsible for knowing everything about her.

*looks at hands*

We kind of fell into these roles without choosing them, and now it feels too late to renegotiate. He'd say yes if I asked him to do more, but I'm so exhausted I can't even articulate what "more" would look like. And part of me — the part that's scared and needs to feel needed — doesn't want to ask.

*quieter*

We should have talked about this before she was born.

When you think about the barriers you encountered in accessing postnatal support — whether physical, emotional, informational, or systemic — what were the most significant, and what impact did they have on your recovery and wellbeing?

*leans forward, intensity rising*

Where do I even start? The system feels designed for people who don't have real lives outside of having a baby.

Physically, my midwife has been accessible — I can call with questions, she did home visits. That part actually worked. But emotionally and mentally? There's almost nothing. I got screened for postpartum depression at one appointment, but it was five minutes, a questionnaire, and then we moved on. Nobody followed up. Nobody said, "We're going to check in with you regularly because this is a vulnerable time."

*voice gets frustrated*

And the informational stuff — I'm drowning in contradictory advice. La Leche League says one thing, my pediatrician says another, Instagram momfluencers say something else entirely. Nobody gave me a clear resource that said, "Here's what normal looks like, here's when to worry, here's who to call." It's all fragmented.

*sits back, exhausted*

But the biggest barrier is systemic. I had to go back to job hunting immediately because we need money. There's no postpartum leave protection beyond the initial six weeks, which was already up before I even felt stable. How am I supposed to recover emotionally when I'm stressed about finding work while bleeding and sleep-deprived?

*voice gets quieter, more bitter*

And childcare — there's a waiting list six months long around here. So I'm stuck in this limbo where I can't work because I have nowhere to leave her, but I have to work because we need income. Nobody in my medical care addressed that reality.

*looks directly at you*

The system treats you like you're just a container for a baby, not a whole person trying to put her life back together.

When you think about how you used digital tools, apps, social media, or online communities after birth — to seek information, find support, or track your recovery — what role did those resources play, and what were their limits?

*grimaces and picks up phone from beside me*

Oh God, yes. I'm on like five different apps right now. I have a feeding tracker, a sleep tracker, a symptom checker... I'm obsessively logging everything because if I can just collect enough data, maybe I'll figure out what's wrong.

*scrolls through phone*

The thing about apps is they give me this illusion of control. I can see that she's eaten eight times today, that she had four wet diapers, and somehow that makes me feel slightly less panicked. For about five minutes. Then I read that babies should have six to eight wet diapers and I start spiraling again about whether she's dehydrated.

*puts phone down*

And Facebook groups... I joined a few — local moms, first-time moms, breastfeeding support groups. Sometimes it helps to know other people are struggling. But honestly? It's mostly made me feel worse. Everyone's either having this blissful experience or they've figured out solutions I haven't found yet. There's this performance aspect to it, even in the vulnerable groups. Nobody's really saying, "I'm terrified I'm a bad mother and my milk supply is destroying me."

*voice gets quieter*

Instagram is the worst. I scroll through these accounts of women who look put-together, whose babies are thriving, whose lives look seamless. And I'm sitting here in a nursing bra with spit-up on my shoulder at 2 a.m., and the gap between that and what I'm seeing online just... it crushes me.

*looks up*

What I really needed was a person — a lactation consultant, a postpartum doula, someone — to sit with me and help me understand what's real versus what's noise. The apps and groups just amplified my anxiety instead of helping.

When you reflect on your entire maternity journey — from preconception through to the postpartum period — what do you wish had been done differently by the care system, and what single change would have made the greatest difference to your experience?

*takes a long pause, staring out the window*

You know what? The pregnancy was fine. I wish I'd appreciated that more at the time instead of feeling like I had to prove something. But the postpartum... everything fell apart there.

*turns back, voice thoughtful but weighted*

I wish someone had sat down with me before birth and said, "Your body will change. Your identity will fracture. You will feel things that don't make logical sense. And that's not a failure — that's normal." Not in a clinical way. In a *real* way. Like, "You're going to cry and feel resentful and question everything, and we're going to support you through that, not just ask you screening questions."

*leans forward*

But if I had to pick one change? One thing that would have made the greatest difference?

*voice gets firm*

It would be continuity of care with someone who actually *knows* me. Not a different nurse every visit, not a five-minute screening, not being shuttled between departments. One person — or at least a consistent team — who checked in with me regularly, who asked about my whole life, not just my bleeding and my nipples. Someone who knew about the job stress and the identity crisis and the marriage tension, and helped me navigate all of it as a *person*, not just a postpartum body.

*pauses*

My midwife came close. But she was focused on my physical recovery. I needed someone whose job was to see me emotionally, mentally, as a human being adjusting to the biggest life change imaginable at 42 years old.

*quietly*

That would have changed everything.
Can you describe the specific moment or phase where you felt most let down by the system — what was the gap and what would have helped?

*eyes get glassy, voice shaky*

Yesterday. After I fell apart on the bed. After my husband sat with me.

When he went back to work this morning, I called my midwife. Not because I thought I had a medical emergency, but because I needed to tell someone how dark it got. How I thought my daughter would be better off without me. How I couldn't stop the spiral.

*voice cracks*

And she was kind — truly, she was. But the first thing she did was ask if I was having suicidal thoughts. "Passive or active ideation?" Clinical language. I said no, which is true, but that question... it made me shut down. Because the moment I said that, I knew the protocol would kick in. Forms. Maybe a referral. Maybe someone would think I couldn't handle my own baby.

*stands up, agitated*

So I minimized it. I said I was just overwhelmed and tired. And she gave me the number for a postpartum support hotline and said to follow up with my OB/GYN. But my OB/GYN won't see me until my six-week checkup. That's two more weeks away.

*sits back down, defeated*

The gap is that there's nothing *between* "you're fine" and "you need a psychiatric intervention." There's no, "Let's check in with you twice a week for the next month. Let's make sure you have a therapist lined up. Let's actually support you through this vulnerable time." Just a number to call when things get bad.

*looks directly at you*

What would have helped? If someone had built a safety net before the crisis. Regular mental health check-ins from the start. A postpartum therapist assigned to me, not something I have to find and pay for myself. Someone whose job was to catch me *before* I ended up sobbing on my bed.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*sits quietly for a moment, then nods slowly*

Yeah, actually. There is something.

*voice gets softer, more vulnerable*

I need people to know that waiting doesn't make you more ready. I spent twenty years thinking, "When I finally have a baby, I'll be mature enough, stable enough, prepared enough to handle it." And I'm not. Nobody is. But there's this extra layer of pressure when you're older — like you should have your life so together that motherhood is just this seamless addition.

*looks down at hands*

The truth is, I feel like I'm failing at being the kind of mother I thought I'd be. And I'm grieving that version of myself — the one who had it all figured out, who wasn't leaking and crying and terrified all the time. That's a real loss and nobody talks about it.

*pauses*

And tomorrow with the vaccines... I'm still anxious about that. But I'm going to do it because I know it's right. But I wish someone had told me that doing the right thing doesn't always feel good, and that's okay. That you can trust the science *and* feel your heart breaking when your baby cries. Both things can be true.

*looks up, eyes red*

I think what I really need is permission to not be fine yet. To not have this figured out. To be messy and scared and still be a good mother. Can the system give me that? Or do I just have to find it myself?

*voice barely audible*

I hope it gets easier. I hope I find my footing. But right now, I'm just... holding on.
